title: DLP Policy Events

compliance.assurance_rationale: HIPAA Privacy Rule §164.502(a) Uses and disclosures of PHI — DLP policy events detect PHI disclosure outside the minimum-necessary standard.
compliance.controlObjective: Detects violations of HIPAA Privacy §164.502(a) (Uses and disclosures of PHI — general rules) — Splunk UC-11.1.6: DLP Policy Events.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-11.1.6' running on O365 DLP logs, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-11.1.6' running on O365 DLP logs, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=o365 sourcetype="ms:o365:management" Workload="Dlp"
| stats count by PolicyName, UserPrincipalName, SensitiveInfoType
| sort -count
description: M365 DLP policy matches across email, Teams, SharePoint identify sensitive data exposure. Centralized tracking supports compliance.
value: M365 DLP policy matches across email, Teams, SharePoint identify sensitive data exposure. Centralized tracking supports compliance.
implementation: Configure M365 DLP policies. Ingest DLP events. Track violations by policy, user, and data type. Alert on high-severity matches. Produce compliance reports for regulated data (PII, PCI, HIPAA).
visualization: Bar chart (violations by policy), Table (top violators), Line chart (violation trend).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_MS_O365`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: O365 DLP logs.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Configure M365 DLP policies. Ingest DLP events. Track violations by policy, user, and data type. Alert on high-severity matches. Produce compliance reports for regulated data (PII, PCI, HIPAA).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=o365 sourcetype="ms:o365:management" Workload="Dlp"
| stats count by PolicyName, UserPrincipalName, SensitiveInfoType
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**DLP Policy Events** — M365 DLP policy matches across email, Teams, SharePoint identify sensitive data exposure. Centralized tracking supports compliance.

Documented **Data sources**: O365 DLP logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_MS_O365`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: o365; **sourcetype**: ms:o365:management. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=o365, sourcetype="ms:o365:management". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by PolicyName, UserPrincipalName, SensitiveInfoType** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (violations by policy), Table (top violators), Line chart (violation trend).
grandmaExplanation: M365 DLP policy matches across email, Teams, SharePoint identify sensitive data exposure — so phishing, data leaks, and account misuse do not slip through.
